## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between a Capture Filter and a Display Filter in Wireshark. Give one example of each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Capture Filter | Display Filter |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Syntax** | BPF (tcpdump-style) | Wireshark proprietary |
> | **When applied** | At ingress, before storage | After storage, during viewing |
> | **Effect on disk** | Reduces file size | No effect on file size |
> | **Performance** | Faster, kernel-level | Slower, userspace |
> | **Example** | `host 10.0.0.5 and tcp port 80` | `ip.addr == 10.0.0.5 && tcp.port == 80` |
> | **Operators** | `and`, `or`, `not` | `&&`, `\|\|`, `!` |
>
> (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for example, 1 for syntax difference)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **What is promiscuous mode? Why is it necessary for packet capture in Wireshark?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (1 mark):** Promiscuous mode is a NIC operational mode where the interface accepts *all* frames traversing the medium, ignoring the destination MAC filter. Normally, a NIC drops frames not addressed to it.
> - **Necessity (1.5 marks):** Without promiscuous mode, the NIC discards frames destined to other hosts, so Wireshark would only see broadcast/multicast + its-own-MAC traffic — useless for analyzing third-party conversations (e.g., between two other machines on the same hub/switch).
> - **Caveat (0.5 mark):** On a switched network, even promiscuous mode cannot capture *other* hosts' unicast traffic — port mirroring (SPAN) or a network tap is required. This is the fundamental reason network admins configure **SPAN ports** on switches.

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Wireshark **three-pane architecture**. Describe the function of each pane. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Perform a step-by-step Wireshark experiment to **capture and analyze a TCP three-way handshake**. What flags and sequence numbers do you observe? How do you confirm the connection is `ESTABLISHED`? **(7 marks)**
>
> ---
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Three-Pane Architecture (7 marks):**
> Refer to the *Wireshark Three-Pane Architecture* diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. Written explanation:
>
> 1. **Packet List Pane (Top) — 2 marks:** Tabular, time-ordered display of every captured packet. Columns include: Packet Number, Time, Source, Destination, Protocol, Length, Info. The Info column is protocol-aware (e.g., *SYN, [SYN ACK], HTTP GET*).
>
> 2. **Packet Details Pane (Middle) — 3 marks:** Hierarchical, collapsible tree view showing each protocol layer's parsed fields. Mirrors the **OSI encapsulation stack**: *Frame → Ethernet II → Internet Protocol Version 4 → Transmission Control Protocol → Hypertext Transfer Protocol*. Clicking any field highlights corresponding bytes in the bottom pane.
>
> 3. **Packet Bytes Pane (Bottom) — 2 marks:** Raw hexadecimal dump (left column) alongside ASCII interpretation (right column). Used for forensic byte-level analysis and to verify dissector correctness.
>
> ---
>
> **(b) TCP Three-Way Handshake Analysis (7 marks):**
>
> 1. **Step 1 (1 mark):** Open browser, type `http://example.com`. Wireshark is capturing in the background. Apply display filter: `tcp.flags.syn == 1` to isolate connection initiations.
>
> 2. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Identify **Packet 1 — SYN**:
>    - Source: Client ephemeral port (e.g., 51234)
>    - Destination: Server port 80
>    - Flags: **SYN=1, ACK=0**
>    - Sequence Number: `Seq = x` (relative, often 0 in modern Wireshark)
>    - Window Size: ~65535
>
> 3. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Identify **Packet 2 — SYN, ACK**:
>    - Source: Server port 80
>    - Destination: Client port
>    - Flags: **SYN=1, ACK=1**
>    - Sequence: `Seq = y`, Acknowledgment: `Ack = x + 1`
>    - Server allocates its side of the connection.
>
> 4. **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Identify **Packet 3 — ACK**:
>    - Source: Client
>    - Flags: **SYN=0, ACK=1**
>    - `Seq = x + 1`, `Ack = y + 1`
>    - **Connection state is now `ESTABLISHED`** in both ends' TCP state machines.
>
> 5. **Step 5 (0.5 mark):** Use `Follow TCP Stream` to see the subsequent HTTP request flowing in this established connection — final proof of successful handshake.

---

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice, 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **protocol dissection** in Wireshark. How does the dissector pipeline handle a frame containing an Ethernet → IP → TCP → HTTP stack? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write the Wireshark display filters to:
>   (i) Show only DNS queries (not responses)
>   (ii) Show only packets containing the word "password" in any HTTP URI
>   (iii) Show only TCP retransmissions
>   (iv) Show all traffic to/from host 192.168.1.10
>   Justify each filter with one line of reasoning. **(7 marks)**
>
> ---
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Protocol Dissection (7 marks):**
> - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Protocol dissection is Wireshark's process of **parsing raw bytes into semantically meaningful, RFC-compliant fields** using a chain of protocol dissectors. Each dissector registers a *decode-as* table and a *heuristic* table.
> - **Pipeline Mechanism (3.5 marks):** When a frame enters Wireshark:
>   1. The **Frame dissector** (always first) timestamps the packet and computes deltas.
>   2. The **Ethernet dissector** reads EtherType (0x0800) → hands off to the **IP dissector**.
>   3. The **IP dissector** reads Protocol field (0x06) → hands off to the **TCP dissector**.
>   4. The **TCP dissector** reads Destination Port (80) → invokes the **HTTP dissector**.
>   5. The **HTTP dissector** parses method, URI, headers, and body.
> - **Reassembly Layer (2 marks):** TCP is a stream protocol, so the dissector must **reassemble segments** into ordered application-layer PDUs before HTTP can interpret them. Wireshark does this transparently using the *PDU Reassembly* table. UDP-based protocols (DNS) need no reassembly since each datagram is a self-contained PDU.
>
> **(b) Filter Construction (7 marks — 1.75 each):**
>
> | # | Filter | Justification |
> |---|---|---|
> | (i) | `dns.flags.response == 0` | The DNS flags field's `QR` bit = 0 indicates a query; = 1 indicates a response. |
> | (ii) | `http.request.uri contains "password"` | The `contains` operator performs case-insensitive substring search on the URI string. |
> | (iii) | `tcp.analysis.retransmission` | Wireshark's TCP analysis engine flags retransmitted segments via this pseudo-field. |
> | (iv) | `ip.addr == 192.168.1.10` | The `ip.addr` shorthand matches *both* source and destination — equivalent to `ip.src == 192.168.1.10 \|\| ip.dst == 192.168.1.10`. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ Confusing **capture filter** syntax with **display filter** syntax in the same answer.
> 2. ❌ Writing `host 192.168.1.10` when asked for a *display* filter — examiners deduct full marks because the question explicitly says "Wireshark display filter."
> 3. ❌ Forgetting to mention that **switches drop unicast traffic not destined to your port** — students naïvely assume promiscuous mode captures everything on a LAN.
> 4. ❌ Saying Wireshark is a "hacking tool" — examiners mark this as **conceptual error**. Wireshark is a *forensic analysis* tool; its legality depends entirely on the **user's authorization**.
> 5. ❌ Failing to differentiate **SYN** from **SYN+ACK** flags in the three-way handshake table.
> 6. ❌ Skipping the **Follow TCP Stream** demonstration — KTU rubrics specifically award 1–2 marks for this step.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam Quick Recall (Wireshark Module 2 — Last-Minute Revision):**
>
> - 🔹 **Wireshark = protocol analyzer**, not a "hacker tool." Requires **libpcap / Npcap**.
> - 🔹 **Two filter languages:** Capture (BPF) vs Display (Wireshark). Know the operators: `and` vs `&&`, `or` vs `\|\|`, `not` vs `!`.
> - 🔹 **Three panes:** Packet List (top), Packet Details (middle), Packet Bytes (bottom).
> - 🔹 **TCP Three-Way Handshake:** SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK. Sequence numbers: `x`, `y`, `x+1`/`y+1`.
> - 🔹 **DNS** is UDP/53; query has `QR=0`, response has `QR=1`.
> - 🔹 **HTTP** runs over TCP/80; HTTPS uses TCP/443 with TLS — Wireshark can decrypt TLS **only if** the session key log (SSLKEYLOGFILE) is provided.
> - 🔹 **Promiscuous mode** is necessary but **not sufficient** on switched networks — SPAN port or hub/tap required.
> - 🔹 **File format:** `.pcapng` is the modern default; `.pcap` is legacy.
> - 🔹 **Color rules:** TCP=light purple, UDP=light blue, DNS=light yellow, HTTP=light green.
> - 🔹 **Follow Stream** = right-click → Follow → TCP/UDP/HTTP/SSL stream.
> - 🔹 **Standard filters to memorize:** `http`, `dns`, `arp`, `icmp`, `tcp.flags.syn == 1`, `tcp.analysis.retransmission`, `ip.addr == x.x.x.x`.
> - 🔹 **Lab record MUST contain:** screenshots of (i) interface selection, (ii) packet list with mixed protocols, (iii) dissected DNS, (iv) TCP handshake, (v) Follow Stream window, (vi) HTTP request/response.
> - 🔹 **Ethical reminder:** Always obtain **written authorization** before capturing on any network you do not own.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network mapping and analysing using Nmap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCL508 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Network mapping and analysing using Nmap ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > **Nmap (Network Mapper)** is an open-source, command-line utility used for **network discovery**, **host enumeration**, **port scanning**, **service and version detection**, **Operating System (OS) fingerprinting**, and **vulnerability analysis** through the Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE). It operates by transmitting specially crafted network packets to a target host and analysing the responses to infer logical network topology and security posture. It is the de-facto industry-standard tool taught in the **KTU Network and System Security Lab (PCCCL508)** under the **Module Experiment 2** of the **2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus for B.Tech Computer Science & Engineering. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Digital Building Inspector"** > > Imagine a large, gated apartment complex at night. > - The **building (target host)** has many flats (**ports** — numbered 1 to 65535). > - You are the **Inspector (Nmap)**. > - You knock on each flat door using different knocking styles: > - A *normal polite knock* (**TCP Connect scan `-sT`**) — completes a full handshake. > - A *half-knock then retreat* (**SYN scan `-sS`**) — fast and stealthy. > - A *no-knock* (**NULL scan `-sN`**) — leaves the door silent. > - A *knock with bells ringing* (**Xmas scan `-sX`**) — sets FIN, PSH, URG flags. > - Based on the **response** (lights turn on / door opens / silence), you deduce which flats are **occupied (open)**, **empty (closed)**, or have a **guard blocking the door (filtered)**. > - You also look at the **type of car parked outside (OS fingerprinting `-O`)** to know the make/model of the resident. This is exactly what Nmap does — it crafts packets, listens to responses, and deduces the **network topology**, **open services**, and **security state** of remote systems. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary (KTU Board High-Yield) | Term | Formal Meaning | |---|---| | **Host Discovery** | Identifying live/active hosts on a network | | **Port Scanning** | Probing a host's ports to determine state | | **Service Version Detection** | Identifying the application and version running on an open port | | **OS Fingerprinting** | Determining the remote operating system via TCP/IP stack behaviour | | **NSE** | Nmap Scripting Engine — Lua-based automation framework | | **Zenmap** | Official Graphical User Interface (GUI) for Nmap | > [!NOTE] > **Six Legal Port States Recognised by Nmap:** > `open`, `closed`, `filtered`, `unfiltered`, `open|filtered`, `closed|filtered`. > Examiners frequently award marks for correctly defining all six — **memorise them**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of an Nmap Scan An Nmap scan, in essence, is a structured five-stage pipeline. Understanding this pipeline is critical for KTU's outcome-based questions (mapped to **CO1: Apply tools for network reconnaissance**). **Stage 1 — Target Enumeration (Input Parsing)** - Nmap accepts targets in **four formats**: hostname, IP address, CIDR range (`192.168.1.0/24`), and IP range (`192.168.1.1-50`). - Reverse DNS resolution is performed (unless `-n` is given) to translate IP → hostname. **Stage 2 — Host Discovery (Ping Sweep)** - Determines which targets are **online** before scanning ports (saves time). - Default uses an **ICMP echo request** + **TCP ACK to port 80** + **TCP SYN to port 443**. - Can be disabled with `-Pn` (treat all hosts as up — used when ICMP is blocked). **Stage 3 — Reverse DNS Resolution** - Resolves the IP addresses of live hosts back to hostnames for reporting. **Stage 4 — Port Scanning (The Core)** - Sends raw packets to a list of ports (default: top 1000) and analyses the response flags. - Each response pattern is mapped to a **port state** (see Section 2.3). **Stage 5 — Script Scanning, Version Detection, OS Detection, Traceroute** - Performed when flags `-sC`, `-sV`, `-O`, or `--traceroute` are used. - Builds a comprehensive profile of the target. ### 2.2 Nmap Scan Techniques — The "Why & How" Matrix | Scan Type | Flag | TCP Flags Sent | Response if Open | Stealth | Privileges | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **SYN (Half-open) Scan** | `-sS` | SYN | SYN/ACK | ★★★★ | Root | | **TCP Connect Scan** | `-sT` | SYN, then full handshake | SYN/ACK → ACK | ★★ | None | | **UDP Scan** | `-sU` | UDP datagram | (often no reply) | ★★★ | Root | | **FIN Scan** | `-sF` | FIN | (no reply) | ★★★★★ | Root | | **NULL Scan** | `-sN` | None | (no reply) | ★★★★★ | Root | | **Xmas Scan** | `-sX` | FIN + PSH + URG | (no reply) | ★★★★★ | Root | | **ACK Scan** | `-sA` | ACK | RST (always) | ★★★★ | Root | | **Window Scan** | `-sW` | ACK | RST | ★★★★ | Root | | **Maimon Scan** | `-sM` | FIN + ACK | (no reply) | ★★★★★ | Root | | **Idle / Zombie Scan** | `-sI ` | Spoofed SYN | SYN/ACK from zombie | ★★★★★ | Root | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Why is a SYN scan called a half-open scan?"* > **Answer:** Because Nmap sends a SYN packet, receives SYN/ACK if the port is open, but **never sends the final ACK** to complete the three-way handshake. The connection is left half-open, making it less likely to be logged by the target application. ### 2.3 The Six Nmap Port States — Definitive Reference | State | Symbolic Meaning | Implication | |---|---|---| | `open` | Application is actively accepting TCP/UDP connections | **Service is running — prime attack surface** | | `closed` | Port responds, but no application is listening | Host is reachable, port is unused | | `filtered` | No response (packet dropped by firewall/ACL) | Cannot determine; packet filtering in place | | `unfiltered` | Port is reachable, but openness cannot be determined (used in ACK scan) | Firewall allows probe through | | `open\|filtered` | No response; could be open OR filtered (UDP, FIN, NULL, Xmas) | Ambiguous — needs further probing | | `closed\|filtered` | No response; could be closed OR filtered (used in IPID idle scan) | Ambiguous — needs further probing | ### 2.4 KTU Command Reference / Cheat Sheet | Purpose | Command Example | |---|---| | Scan a single host | `nmap 192.168.1.10` | | Scan a subnet (CIDR) | `nmap 192.168.1.0/24` | | Scan a range | `nmap 192.168.1.1-50` | | Host discovery only (no port scan) | `nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24` | | Disable host discovery (scan all) | `nmap -Pn 192.168.1.10` | | Specific port(s) | `nmap -p 80,443 192.168.1.10` | | Port range | `nmap -p 1-1024 192.168.1.10` | | All 65535 ports | `nmap -p- 192.168.1.10` | | TCP SYN scan (default with root) | `sudo nmap -sS 192.168.1.10` | | TCP Connect scan (no root) | `nmap -sT 192.168.1.10` | | UDP scan | `sudo nmap -sU 192.168.1.10` | | Version detection | `nmap -sV 192.168.1.10` | | OS detection | `sudo nmap -O 192.168.1.10` | | Aggressive scan (OS + Version + Scripts + Trace) | `sudo nmap -A 192.168.1.10` | | Service & default scripts | `nmap -sC -sV 192.168.1.10` | | NSE script by category | `nmap --script=vuln 192.168.1.10` | | NSE script by name | `nmap --script=http-title 192.168.1.10` | | Timing template (0–5) | `nmap -T4 192.168.1.10` | | Verbose output | `nmap -v 192.168.1.10` | | Save normal output | `nmap -oN scan.txt 192.168.1.10` | | Save XML output | `nmap -oX scan.xml 192.168.1.10` | | Save greppable output | `nmap -oG scan.gnmap 192.168.1.10` | | Save in all three formats | `nmap -oA scan_basename 192.168.1.10` | | Decoy scan (evasion) | `nmap -D RND:10 192.168.1.10` | | Source port spoofing | `nmap --source-port 53 192.168.1.10` | | Fragment packets | `nmap -f 192.168.1.10` | | IPv6 scan | `nmap -6 target_hostname` | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Nmap is not merely a lab tool — it is used in industry for: - **Penetration Testing (PTES & OWASP methodologies)** — pre-engagement reconnaissance. - **Asset Inventory & Compliance Auditing** (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001 require periodic network scans). - **Incident Response (IR)** — verifying which services were exposed during a breach. - **DevOps / Network Monitoring** — verifying firewall rule changes in CI/CD pipelines. - **Academic Research** — Internet-wide measurement studies (e.g., the famous *Censys* and *Shodan* projects use scan engines conceptually similar to Nmap). > [!NOTE] > The Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) allows **Lua-based automation**, enabling custom vulnerability checks. The **default category** contains ~110 scripts, the **vuln category** is the most commonly used in penetration testing, and the **safe category** is preferred in production environments to avoid denial-of-service side effects. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs ### 3.1 Walkthrough — How SYN Scan (`-sS`) Infers an "open" Port This is the most analytically examined Nmap process in KTU examinations. We trace every packet. **Step 1: Initial State** Nmap chooses target IP $T$ and target port $p$. A fresh **raw socket** is opened (requires root/CAP_NET_RAW). **Step 2: Nmap Sends a TCP SYN Packet** Crafted packet has the following header field values: $$ \text{TCP Header: } \{\text{SourcePort}=R,\ \text{DestPort}=p,\ \text{Flags}=SYN,\ \text{SeqNum}=x\} $$ where $R$ is a random ephemeral source port chosen by Nmap and $x$ is an Initial Sequence Number (ISN). **Step 3: Three Possible Responses from the Target** **Case A — Port is OPEN** The target's kernel responds: $$ \text{Response: } \{\text{Flags}=SYN+ACK,\ \text{SeqNum}=y,\ \text{AckNum}=x+1\} $$ Nmap observes the SYN+ACK → concludes `open`. Crucially, Nmap **immediately sends a RST** to tear down the half-open connection (so the application log does not record it). $$ \text{Nmap RST: } \{\text{Flags}=RST,\ \text{SeqNum}=x+1\} $$ **Case B — Port is CLOSED** The target's kernel responds with a RST: $$ \text{Response: } \{\text{Flags}=RST,\ \text{AckNum}=x+1\} $$ Nmap concludes `closed`. **Case C — Port is FILTERED (Firewall drops)** No response is received. After a timeout $\tau$ (governed by the timing template, e.g., `-T4` → $\tau \approx 1\text{s}$), Nmap concludes `filtered`. **Step 4: Final State Update** The Nmap state machine updates its internal table and moves to the next port in the scan list. **Summary Decision Logic (Boolean):** $$ \text{State}(p) = \begin{cases} \text{open} & \text{if } \text{TCP.Flags} \supseteq \{SYN, ACK\} \\ \text{closed} & \text{if } \text{TCP.Flags} \supseteq \{RST\} \\ \text{filtered} & \text{if } \text{timeout after } \tau \end{cases} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the timeout $\tau$ matters:** Nmap's timing templates (`-T0` Paranoid → `-T5` Insane) directly set $\tau$ and the **parallelism** (number of sockets). For `-T0`, $\tau$ can be **300 seconds**, making scans slow but stealthy. For `-T5`, $\tau \approx 0.3\text{s}$, making scans fast but easily detected by IDS. ### 3.2 Walkthrough — A Complete Practical Lab Session (as a KTU student would perform) This is the procedure a student would follow in the **PCCCL508 lab record** for Experiment 2. **Step 1: Identify Your Own Network Range** Use the `ip a` (Linux) or `ipconfig` (Windows) command to find the local subnet. **Step 2: Host Discovery on the Local Subnet** Execute: $$ \texttt{nmap\ -sn\ 192.168.1.0/24} $$ The `-sn` flag tells Nmap to perform **only a ping sweep** and skip the port scan. Output lists all live hosts (typically via ARP for local subnets when Nmap detects a LAN). **Step 3: Detailed Scan of a Discovered Host** Pick one target (say `192.168.1.15`): $$ \texttt{sudo\ nmap\ -sS\ -sV\ -O\ -A\ 192.168.1.15} $$ Expected output sections: 1. **Nmap scan report** (host summary, latency). 2. **PORT / STATE / SERVICE / VERSION** table. 3. **OS details** (e.g., `Running: Linux 5.x`, `CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel`). 4. **Network Distance**: hop count. 5. **NSE script results** (e.g., `http-title`, `ssh-hostkey`). **Step 4: Run a Targeted Vulnerability Script** $$ \texttt{nmap\ --script=vuln\ -p\ 80,443\ 192.168.1.15} $$ **Step 5: Export the Report** $$ \texttt{nmap\ -A\ -oA\ labreport\_exp2\ 192.168.1.15} $$ This produces `labreport_exp2.nmap`, `labreport_exp2.xml`, and `labreport_exp2.gnmap` — the trio required for KTU lab submissions. ### 3.3 Walkthrough — Analysing a Sample Nmap Output (Board Exam Style) Consider the following truncated output: ``` PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 8.2p1 Ubuntu 80/tcp open http Apache httpd 2.4.41 443/tcp closed https 8080/tcp filtered http-proxy ``` **Interpretation (Model Answer for KTU):** > "Host `192.168.1.15` is running **Ubuntu Linux** (inferred from OpenSSH banner `Ubuntu 8.2p1`). Port 22 is **open** and running **OpenSSH 8.2p1**, providing secure remote access. Port 80 is **open** with **Apache 2.4.41**, indicating an active web server. Port 443 is **closed** — HTTPS is not configured. Port 8080 is **filtered**, meaning a firewall or ACL is silently dropping packets destined for it, and further probing (e.g., `-sV` or ACK scan) is required to determine if a service is hidden behind the filter. Security recommendation: filter the SSH banner to avoid OS disclosure, and investigate why an unexpected port 8080 is present." --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Nmap Scanning Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture This Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end execution flow** of an Nmap scan, from CLI input to final report generation. It is the **block-level functional architecture** referenced in the KTU board pattern for instrumentation-style lab questions. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User CLI Input
nmap target flags] --> B[Target Parser
Hostname IP CIDR Range] B --> C[Host Discovery Engine
ICMP TCP ACK SYN ARP] C --> D{Is Host Alive} D -- No --> E[Mark Host as Down
Skip Port Scan] D -- Yes --> F[Reverse DNS Lookup
IP to Hostname] F --> G[Port Selection Module
Top 1000 Specific p- All] G --> H[Raw Packet Crafter
Build SYN FIN UDP Probes] H --> I[Network Transmission
Send Packets to Target] I --> J[Response Analyzer
Parse TCP Flags TTL IPID] J --> K[Port State Classifier
open closed filtered unfiltered] K --> L{Optional Modules} L -- O Flag --> M[OS Fingerprinting Engine
Match TCP IP Stack] L -- sV Flag --> N[Version Detection Engine
Probe and Parse Banners] L -- Script Flag --> O[NSE Script Runner
Lua Interpreter] L -- Traceroute --> P[Traceroute Engine
TTL Analysis] M --> Q[Report Aggregator] N --> Q O --> Q P --> Q K --> Q Q --> R[Output Formatter
Normal XML Greppable] R --> S[Final Report
stdout file] ``` ### 4.2 TCP Three-Way Handshake vs. Nmap SYN Half-Open — Comparative Diagram This second diagram visually contrasts a **legitimate TCP handshake** with Nmap's **stealthy SYN scan**, clarifying *why* a half-open scan evades application-layer logging. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant C as Nmap Client participant S as Target Server rect rgb(220, 245, 220) Note over C,S: LEGITIMATE TCP CONNECTION (Connect Scan sT) C->>S: SYN seq x S-->>C: SYN ACK seq y ack x+1 C->>S: ACK seq x+1 ack y+1 Note over C,S: Connection Established, App Logged end rect rgb(255, 230, 230) Note over C,S: NMAP STEALTHY SYN SCAN (sS) C->>S: SYN seq x S-->>C: SYN ACK seq y ack x+1 C->>S: RST seq x+1 Note over C,S: Connection Torn Down, Half-Open Note over S: Application Log shows NOTHING end rect rgb(230, 230, 250) Note over C,S: FILTERED PORT RESPONSE C->>S: SYN seq x Note over S: Firewall DROPS packet Note over C: Timeout State equals filtered end ``` ### 4.3 Port State Decision Tree — Mermaid Block Diagram This diagram encodes the **decision logic** for classifying each probed port. It is exactly the kind of flowchart examiners expect for **3-mark "explain with diagram" questions**. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Probe Sent to Port p] --> Q1{Response Received} Q1 -- No Response --> Q2{Scan Type} Q2 -- UDP FIN NULL Xmas --> S1[State: open or filtered] Q2 -- Other --> S2[State: filtered] Q1 -- Yes RST --> S3{ACK Scan Mode} S3 -- Yes --> S4[State: unfiltered] S3 -- No --> S5[State: closed] Q1 -- Yes SYN ACK --> S6[State: open] S1 --> Q3[Run Version Probe to Disambiguate] Q3 -- Banner Returned --> S6 Q3 -- No Banner --> S2 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]** > *List and briefly explain any **six port states** recognised by Nmap.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The six port states defined by Nmap are: 1. **open** — An application is actively accepting connections on this port. 2. **closed** — The port is reachable and responds, but no application is listening. 3. **filtered** — Packet filtering (firewall) prevents Nmap from determining the state. 4. **unfiltered** — The port is reachable, but openness cannot be determined (typical of ACK scans). 5. **open|filtered** — Nmap cannot decide between open and filtered (UDP, FIN, NULL, Xmas scans). 6. **closed|filtered** — Nmap cannot decide between closed and filtered (used in IPID idle scans). *(1 mark for naming all six; 1 mark for any two clear definitions; 1 mark for the contextual scan type.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** > *What is the difference between a **TCP Connect scan (`-sT`)** and a **SYN scan (`-sS`)**? Why is SYN scan considered stealthier?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **TCP Connect scan (`-sT`)** uses the OS's high-level `connect()` system call to perform a full three-way handshake (SYN → SYN/ACK → ACK) and then closes the connection politely with FIN. It **does not require root privileges** but is logged by the target application. A **SYN scan (`-sS`)** sends a raw SYN packet, observes the SYN/ACK response if the port is open, and **immediately sends a RST** to abort the connection — *never completing the handshake*. It requires **root privileges** (raw sockets) and is **stealthier** because most application-level logging only happens after the handshake completes, so the half-open probe goes unlogged. *(3 marks: 1 for connect scan description, 1 for SYN scan description, 1 for stealth justification.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- > ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]** > > **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **Nmap scanning architecture** and the function of each stage in the scan pipeline. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Perform a **detailed comparative analysis** of the following scan types, mentioning the TCP flags used, the expected response for an open and closed port, the stealth rating, and the privilege requirement: `SYN scan, FIN scan, NULL scan, Xmas scan, ACK scan, Idle scan`. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Nmap Scanning Architecture (7 Marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** (the Mermaid pipeline diagram) for the full architecture. The pipeline consists of: 1. **Target Parser** — accepts hostname, IP, CIDR, or IP range. 2. **Host Discovery Engine** — uses ICMP echo, TCP SYN/ACK to ports 80/443, and ARP for LANs. 3. **Reverse DNS Resolver** — converts IPs to hostnames. 4. **Port Selection Module** — chooses the top 1000 ports, a user-specified set, or all 65535 (`-p-`). 5. **Raw Packet Crafter** — builds the probes (e.g., SYN with random source port). 6. **Network Transmitter** — sends packets using raw sockets. 7. **Response Analyzer** — parses incoming packets, examines TCP flags, TTL, IPID, window size. 8. **Port State Classifier** — maps responses to one of the six states. 9. **Optional Modules** — OS detection (`-O`), version detection (`-sV`), NSE scripts (`--script`), and traceroute. 10. **Report Aggregator & Output Formatter** — formats results as normal, XML, or greppable output. *(Allocate ~0.7 marks per stage; the diagram in Section 4.1 serves as the "neat labelled diagram" requirement.)* **(b) Comparative Table of Scan Types (7 Marks):** | Scan | Flag | TCP Flags Sent | Open Port Response | Closed Port Response | Stealth | Root? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | SYN | `-sS` | SYN | SYN+ACK | RST | ★★★★ | Yes | | FIN | `-sF` | FIN | (no reply) | RST | ★★★★★ | Yes | | NULL | `-sN` | none | (no reply) | RST | ★★★★★ | Yes | | Xmas | `-sX` | FIN+PSH+URG | (no reply) | RST | ★★★★★ | Yes | | ACK | `-sA` | ACK | RST | RST | ★★★★ | Yes | | Idle | `-sI ` | Spoofed SYN via zombie | SYN+ACK from zombie (IPID+2) | RST or no reply (IPID+1) | ★★★★★ | Yes | *For each row, allocate 1 mark for flags + open/closed response; the final mark is reserved for the privilege/stealth synthesis discussion.* --- > ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** > > **(a)** Explain the **Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE)** in detail. Discuss its **architecture**, **script categories** (mention any four), and write a sample command to run a vulnerability scan against a web server on a target host. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** A system administrator runs the following command on a host with IP `10.0.0.5`: > `sudo nmap -sS -sV -O -A -p 1-1000 -oA results 10.0.0.5` > **Explain each flag in detail, describe the three output files generated, and interpret a sample output showing the following ports:** *22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 7.6, 80/tcp open http Apache 2.4.29, 3306/tcp filtered mysql.* *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) NSE Architecture and Script Categories (7 Marks):** The **Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE)** is a Lua-embedded automation framework that extends Nmap's capabilities beyond simple port scanning. Its architecture consists of: - **Lua interpreter** linked into Nmap. - **Script library** located at `/usr/share/nmap/scripts/` (over 600 scripts on a default install). - **NSE API** that allows scripts to access host information (open ports, versions, OS) discovered during the scan. - **Parallel execution engine** — runs all selected scripts concurrently after the main scan. **Four Key Script Categories:** 1. **auth** — scripts that test authentication credentials (e.g., `ftp-anon`). 2. **default** — safe scripts run when `-sC` is used. 3. **vuln** — actively checks for known vulnerabilities (e.g., `http-vuln-cve*`). 4. **safe** — scripts that are unlikely to crash the target (preferred in production). **Sample Vulnerability Scan Command:** $$ \texttt{sudo\ nmap\ --script=vuln\ -p\ 80,443\ 10.0.0.5} $$ This command runs the entire **vuln category** of NSE scripts against ports 80 and 443 of the target. *(1 mark per category × 4 = 4 marks; 2 marks for architecture; 1 mark for sample command.)* **(b) Flag-by-Flag Explanation & Output Interpretation (7 Marks):** | Flag | Meaning | |---|---| | `sudo` | Execute as superuser — required for raw socket access. | | `nmap` | Invokes the Network Mapper tool. | | `-sS` | TCP SYN stealth scan (half-open). | | `-sV` | Enable service and version detection (probes open ports and matches banners). | | `-O` | Enable OS detection via TCP/IP stack fingerprinting. | | `-A` | Aggressive mode — combines `-sV`, `-O`, default script scan, and traceroute. | | `-p 1-1000` | Scan ports 1 through 1000 (default range, made explicit). | | `-oA results` | Save output in **all three formats** with the basename `results`. | **Three Output Files Generated:** 1. `results.nmap` — Human-readable normal text output. 2. `results.xml` — Structured XML (for programmatic parsing by tools like Metasploit). 3. `results.gnmap` — Greppable format (one line per host, suitable for shell pipelines). **Interpretation of the Sample Output (3 Marks):** - **22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 7.6** → SSH service is active; banner disclosure reveals the **exact version (7.6)** and a likely OS hint (OpenSSH 7.6 is associated with Debian/Ubuntu 18.04 era). *Security risk: outdated version susceptible to known CVEs.* - **80/tcp open http Apache 2.4.29** → Web server is active, running an **older Apache version (2.4.29)** with publicly known vulnerabilities. *Security risk: must update to the latest 2.4.x patch.* - **3306/tcp filtered mysql** → Port 3306 (MySQL) is **not directly reachable**; a firewall/ACL is silently dropping the probe. Nmap cannot determine if MySQL is running. *Security risk: requires further probing (`-sV -Pn --script=mysql-info`) to confirm or rule out the service.* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (as observed in KTU valuation):** > 1. **Confusing `open` with `open|filtered`** — students often mark UDP `open|filtered` ports as simply `open`. This loses 0.5–1 mark per occurrence. > 2. **Forgetting that `-sS` requires root** — if a question asks "which scan can be run as a non-privileged user?", the answer is **only `-sT`** (TCP Connect). Mentioning `-sS` here is a 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Wrong flag for "no ping"** — students write `-sP` (deprecated) instead of the correct **`-Pn`**. Validators explicitly check for the modern flag. > 4. **Missing the stealth logic in SYN scan** — simply saying "SYN scan is faster" is incomplete. The correct justification is *both* speed **and** evasion of application-layer logging. > 5. **Not citing the three output formats** when `-oA` is mentioned — examiners award 1 mark for naming `.nmap`, `.xml`, and `.gnmap`. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (print this before the exam):** > > - **Nmap = Network Mapper**, open-source, by Gordon Lyon (Fyodor). Used for **network discovery** and **security auditing**. > - **Six port states**: `open`, `closed`, `filtered`, `unfiltered`, `open|filtered`, `closed|filtered`. > - **Default scan (with root)** = `-sS` (SYN / half-open). **Default (no root)** = `-sT` (TCP Connect). > - **`-sS` is half-open** because it sends SYN, gets SYN/ACK, then **sends RST** — never completes the handshake. > - **`-sU`** = UDP scan, **slow** (because most UDP ports are `open|filtered`), often combined with `-sS`. > - **`-sV`** = version detection (probes banners). **`-O`** = OS detection (TCP/IP fingerprint). > - **`-A`** = aggressive (combines `-sV`, `-O`, `-sC`, `--traceroute`). > - **`-Pn`** = skip host discovery (treat all hosts as up). **`-sn`** = host discovery only, no port scan. > - **Timing templates**: `-T0` (paranoid, slow) → `-T5` (insane, fast). > - **Three output formats** via `-oA basename`: `.nmap` (text), `.xml` (structured), `.gnmap` (greppable). > - **NSE** uses Lua scripts; common categories: `default`, `vuln`, `safe`, `auth`, `discovery`, `exploit`. > - **Zenmap** is the official GUI for Nmap — useful for **profile-based** scans and **topology visualisation**. > - **Stealth hierarchy** (most → least): Idle > FIN/NULL/Xmas > SYN > TCP Connect > UDP. > - **Evasion techniques**: `-f` (fragment), `-D` (decoy), `--source-port` (sport spoofing), `--mtu` (custom MTU). > - **Always remember**: scanning networks **without written authorisation is illegal** under the IT Act 2000 (India) and equivalent international laws (CFAA in the USA, Computer Misuse Act in the UK). Use only on the KTU lab subnet or your own test hosts.

topics.title: Port scanning using Angry IP Scanner or Advanced IP Scanner
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCL508 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Port scanning using Angry IP Scanner or Advanced IP Scanner # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Port Scanning? **Port scanning** is the systematic probing of a target host's network ports to determine which ports are **open, closed, or filtered**. It is a foundational reconnaissance technique used in both legitimate network administration and penetration testing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (as per 2024 Scheme):** Port scanning is a network surveillance technique that sends carefully crafted packets to a target host's Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) or User Datagram Protocol (UDP) port numbers with the objective of identifying the available services, running versions, and operating systems on a target machine. **Conceptual Analogy (Building Security Check):** Imagine a large office building with hundreds of doors. A security guard (the *scanner*) walks around checking each door (the *port*) to see if it's: - **Open** — somebody is inside and responding - **Closed** — the door is locked but reachable - **Filtered** — a guard dog barks before you can tell Port scanning is the network equivalent of this door-to-door check, helping the administrator know *which services* are reachable on a target machine. ## 1.2 Angry IP Scanner — The Tool **Angry IP Scanner** (also known as *ipscan*) is a **lightweight, open-source, cross-platform network scanner** released under GPL v2. It pings each IP address in a defined range to check if it is alive, then resolves hostnames, scans ports, and retrieves optional **NetBIOS** and **HTTP** information. | Property | Specification | |---|---| | **Developer** | Anton Keks (original), community maintained | | **License** | GPL v2 (free and open-source) | | **Platforms** | Windows, Linux, macOS | | **Engine** | Multi-threaded (very fast) | | **File size** | ~**3–4 MB** (no installation required) | ## 1.3 Advanced IP Scanner — The Alternative **Advanced IP Scanner** is a **free, Windows-only** network scanner from Famatech (Radmin). It is widely used in **small office and home office (SOHO)** networks for discovering and managing LAN devices. | Property | Specification | |---|---| | **Developer** | Famatech (Radmin) | | **License** | Freeware (proprietary) | | **Platforms** | Windows only | | **Engine** | Single/limited threads | | **Special feature** | **Remote control** via Radmin integration | > [!NOTE] > Both tools perform a similar job. KTU accepts **either** tool for this experiment. The *Angry IP Scanner* is preferred for its portability and Linux support, while *Advanced IP Scanner* is preferred when remote desktop access is needed for further testing. ## 1.4 Port States (Standard for all scanners) Every port found during a scan is in one of three states: - **Open** — The target is actively accepting connections on this port (e.g., web server on **80**, SSH on **22**). - **Closed** — The port is reachable but no application is listening. - **Filtered** — A firewall is blocking the probe, so the scanner cannot determine the state. ## 1.5 Why Port Scanning Matters (Engineering Utility) - **Network Auditing:** Admins verify only intended services are exposed. - **Vulnerability Assessment:** Identifies outdated services that may be exploited. - **Penetration Testing:** First step in the **reconnaissance** phase of an engagement. - **Compliance:** Required by standards like **PCI-DSS**, **ISO 27001**, and **NIST SP 800-115**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 The TCP/IP Conceptual Backbone Before running the scanner, the student must understand what is being scanned. The **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** uses a **16-bit port number** field in its header, allowing port numbers from **0 to 65535**. $$\text{Number of available ports} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$$ These are divided by IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority) into: | Port Range | Category | Common Services | |---|---|---| | **0 – 1023** | **Well-Known Ports** | HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), FTP (21), SSH (22), SMTP (25), DNS (53) | | **1024 – 49151** | **Registered Ports** | MySQL (3306), RDP (3389), VNC (5900) | | **49152 – 65535** | **Dynamic / Ephemeral Ports** | Used by clients as source ports | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** For this experiment, you only need to know a handful of well-known ports: **21 (FTP), 22 (SSH), 23 (Telnet), 25 (SMTP), 53 (DNS), 80 (HTTP), 110 (POP3), 443 (HTTPS), 3389 (RDP)**. ## 2.2 How the Scanner Operates (The "Why" and "How") A port scanner is fundamentally a **packet-sending application**. It uses raw or semi-raw sockets to: 1. **Resolve** the target hostname or IP range. 2. **Send** a probe packet to each (IP, port) pair. Angry IP Scanner sends a **TCP SYN** packet by default. 3. **Listen** for the response: - **SYN-ACK** → Port is **OPEN**. - **RST** → Port is **CLOSED**. - **No reply / ICMP unreachable** → Port is **FILTERED**. 4. **Display** the discovered state in a tabular GUI. ## 2.3 Scanning Techniques — The "How" Behind the Curtain | Technique | Packet Sent | Used By | Detection Risk | |---|---|---|---| | **TCP Connect Scan** | Full 3-way handshake | Nmap (`-sT`), most basic tools | High (logged by target) | | **SYN Scan (Stealth)** | SYN only | Nmap (`-sS`) | Low (often called "half-open") | | **FIN Scan** | FIN flag | Nmap (`-sF`) | Very low | | **NULL Scan** | No flags | Nmap (`-sN`) | Very low | | **XMAS Scan** | FIN+PSH+URG | Nmap (`-sX`) | Low | | **UDP Scan** | UDP packet | Nmap (`-sU`) | Slow, unreliable | > [!NOTE] > **Angry IP Scanner and Advanced IP Scanner primarily use the TCP Connect scan method**, which is the simplest and most compatible (does not require administrator privileges on most OS). For pure stealth scanning, the student would graduate to **Nmap**. ## 2.4 KTU Reference Sheet (Cheat-Style Summary) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **IP** | Internet Protocol address, e.g., `192.168.1.10` | | **Port** | A 16-bit number (0–65535) identifying a service | | **Socket** | The combination of `IP:Port` | | **Ping / ICMP Echo** | Used to check if a host is alive | | **Hostname** | Human-readable name mapped to an IP via DNS | | **NetBIOS** | Windows networking protocol for name resolution | | **TTL** | Time-To-Live, decrements by 1 per router hop | | **MAC Address** | 48-bit physical address of the network interface | | **Radmin** | Remote administration tool bundled with Advanced IP Scanner | | **GPL v2** | Open-source license guaranteeing user freedom | | **IDS / IPS** | Intrusion Detection/Prevention System — detects port scans | > [!WARNING] > The KTU lab exam frequently asks: *"Which protocol does Angry IP Scanner use to determine if a host is alive?"* — The correct answer is **ICMP Echo Request (Ping)**. If the host blocks ICMP, the scanner will still try a **TCP port 80** connection as a fallback. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **SOC Analysts (Security Operations Center):** Use similar tools to verify exposed attack surfaces. - **Network Administrators:** Use scanners daily to map their own infrastructure. - **Ethical Hackers (CEH, OSCP):** Port scanning is the **Step 1** of the **5-phase ethical hacking methodology**: *Reconnaissance → Scanning → Gaining Access → Maintaining Access → Clearing Tracks*. - **DevOps Engineers:** Use scanning in CI/CD pipelines to verify container ports. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Walkthrough ## 3.1 Installing the Tool (Angry IP Scanner) **Step 1 — Download the binary** Visit the official source `angryip.org` and download the version matching your OS (Windows `.exe` or Linux `.deb`/`.tar.gz`). **Step 2 — Install on Windows** Double-click the `.exe` file. **No installation is required** — it is a portable application. The executable runs directly from the folder. **Step 3 — Install on Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)** ```bash sudo dpkg -i ipscan_3.9.1_amd64.deb sudo apt-get install -f ``` **Step 4 — Grant Java Runtime (if missing)** Angry IP Scanner is written in Java. If Java is not installed: ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install default-jre ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Java?** Angry IP Scanner uses the *Java Network Launch Protocol* for cross-platform support. The current version (3.x) bundles JRE for Windows but Linux users need a system JRE. ## 3.2 Configuring the Scan — Analytical Breakdown **Step 5 — Launch the application** Open Angry IP Scanner. The main window shows a **three-tab interface**: *Scanning*, *Tools*, *Favorites*. **Step 6 — Define the IP range** The tool requires three input fields: | Field | Example | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **IP Range (Start)** | `192.168.1.1` | First IP in the scan range | | **IP Range (End)** | `192.168.1.254` | Last IP in the scan range | | **Hostname (optional)** | `scanme.nmap.org` | Alternative to IP range (DNS resolved automatically) | **Step 7 — Configure the port list** Click **Tools → Preferences → Ports**. In the **Port Selection** tab, choose: - **Use a specific port range**: e.g., `1-1024` to scan the well-known ports. - **Use a custom list**: e.g., `21,22,80,443,3389` to focus on common services. **Step 8 — Choose the fetchers (plugins)** In the *Fetchers* tab, enable the columns you want displayed: - **PING** — Is the host alive? (ICMP) - **Hostname** — DNS resolution - **Port [X]** — Check if port X is open - **HTTP** — Pull the HTTP banner (web server version) - **NetBIOS** — Windows name resolution **Step 9 — Start the scan** Click the **Start** button. The progress bar shows completion. Multi-threading means a `/24` network scan completes in seconds. ## 3.3 Interpreting the Results — The Analytical Part After completion, the result table displays a row per host: | IP | Ping | Hostname | Port 22 | Port 80 | Port 443 | MAC | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 192.168.1.1 | Yes | router.local | Closed | **Open** | **Open** | AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF | | 192.168.1.10 | Yes | pc-ravi | **Open** | Closed | Closed | 11:22:33:44:55:66 | | 192.168.1.15 | No | — | — | — | — | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the table:** A blank cell (e.g., the 3rd row above) means the host did not respond to **ICMP**, so no port scan was attempted. A cell showing "**Open**" in **bold** means a TCP connection was established. "Closed" means a RST was received. ## 3.4 The OS Detection Inference (Manual) Although Angry IP Scanner does not perform active OS fingerprinting, the student can **infer** the OS from **TTL** values: $$\text{Initial TTL} \approx \text{Observed TTL} + \text{Hop Count}$$ | OS Family | Default Initial TTL | |---|---| | **Windows** | **128** | | **Linux / macOS** | **64** | | **Cisco IOS / Network Devices** | **255** | For example, if `ping` returns a TTL of `64` and the host is 3 hops away, the device is almost certainly **Linux/macOS**. ## 3.5 Advanced IP Scanner — Procedure Adaptations The workflow is similar, but Advanced IP Scanner adds two unique steps: **Step 1 — Run as Administrator** Required so that the tool can access NetBIOS, MAC, and remote service data on Windows hosts. **Step 2 — Use the "Radmin" button** After scanning, right-click any host and select **"Radmin" → "Remote Control"** to launch a remote desktop session. This is **not available in Angry IP Scanner**. **Step 3 — Export results** Click **File → Save Report** to produce a `.csv` of the scan — useful for KTU lab records. ## 3.6 Complete Procedure Summary (For Lab Record) 1. Note the IP address of your own system (`ipconfig` / `ifconfig`). 2. Note the IP range of the LAN (`192.168.x.0/24`). 3. Install and launch **Angry IP Scanner**. 4. Enter the IP range `192.168.x.1` to `192.168.x.254`. 5. In *Preferences*, set the port range to `1-1024` (or custom list). 6. Enable fetchers: **Ping, Hostname, MAC, HTTP, NetBIOS**. 7. Click **Start** and wait for the scan to complete. 8. **Screenshot the result table** — required for the lab record. 9. Identify the **router (port 80/443 open)**, the **file server (ports 21/445 open)**, and **unauthorized devices**. 10. Compare with results from `nmap -sT 192.168.x.0/24` (terminal cross-verification). 11. Document your findings in the **Observation** and **Result** sections of the lab record. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Tool Workflow Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Launches Angry IP Scanner] --> B[Input IP Range or Hostname] B --> C[Read Config: Ports, Fetchers, Threads] C --> D[Resolve DNS for Hostnames] D --> E[Send ICMP Ping to Each IP] E --> F{Host Alive?} F -- No --> Z[Mark as Dead, Skip] F -- Yes --> G[For Each Port in Port List] G --> H[Send TCP SYN Packet] H --> I{Response Type} I -- SYN-ACK --> J[Mark Port as OPEN] I -- RST --> K[Mark Port as CLOSED] I -- No Reply --> L[Mark Port as FILTERED] J --> M[Update Result Table Row] K --> M L --> M M --> N{More Ports?} N -- Yes --> G N -- No --> O{More IPs?} O -- Yes --> E O -- No --> P[Display Final Scan Table] P --> Q[User Exports CSV or Screenshots] ``` ## 4.2 Hierarchical Port Categorization (Mermaid Mindmap) ```mermaid mindmap root((TCP/UDP Ports 0-65535)) Well Known 80 HTTP 443 HTTPS 22 SSH 21 FTP 25 SMTP 53 DNS Registered 3306 MySQL 3389 RDP 5900 VNC 8080 Alt HTTP Dynamic 49152 to 65535 Ephemeral Client Ports ``` ## 4.3 Lab Topology (Typical KTU Lab Network) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LabNetwork[KTU Computer Lab LAN] R[Router / Gateway
192.168.1.1] PC1[Student PC-1
192.168.1.10] PC2[Student PC-2
192.168.1.11] PC3[Student PC-3
192.168.1.12] SRV[Lab Server
192.168.1.5] end A[Scanner Host
192.168.1.10] -.ICMP Ping.-> R A -.TCP SYN.-> PC2 A -.TCP SYN.-> PC3 A -.TCP SYN.-> SRV R --- PC1 R --- PC2 R --- PC3 R --- SRV ``` ## 4.4 Three-Way Handshake vs Stealth (SYN) Scan Comparison ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant S as Scanner participant T as Target Port Note over S,T: TCP Connect Scan (Full Handshake) S->>T: SYN T-->>S: SYN-ACK S->>T: ACK Note over S,T: Port is now LOGGED by the target Note over S,T: Stealth SYN Scan (Angry IP default-style) S->>T: SYN T-->>S: SYN-ACK S->>T: RST (closes without ACK) Note over S,T: Half-open, less likely to be logged ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Define port scanning. List any two port scanning tools.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Port scanning is the technique of probing a target host's TCP or UDP ports to identify which services are running, the state of those ports (open, closed, filtered), and basic system information. **(1.5 marks)** Two popular port scanning tools are: **(0.75 marks each)** 1. **Angry IP Scanner** (cross-platform, open-source) 2. **Advanced IP Scanner** (Windows-only, freeware) ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the role of ICMP Echo Request in Angry IP Scanner?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Angry IP Scanner uses **ICMP Echo Request** (commonly called **Ping**) as the first step of its scanning process to determine whether a target host is **alive or reachable** on the network. **(2 marks)** If the host responds with an **ICMP Echo Reply**, the scanner then proceeds to scan its ports; otherwise, the host is marked as dead and skipped. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice ### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** Explain the concept of port scanning. Discuss the three possible states of a port after a scan. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat workflow, explain how Angry IP Scanner is used to perform a port scan on a local network. List the well-known port numbers for **HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, FTP, and DNS**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Port Scanning — Concept & States (7 marks)** Port scanning is a **reconnaissance technique** used to identify open, closed, and filtered ports on a target host. It works by sending **TCP or UDP packets** to a range of port numbers and analysing the responses. **Three possible port states:** 1. **Open (3 marks):** The target is actively accepting incoming connections on this port. An application or service is listening. Example: A web server on port **80** will respond with a **SYN-ACK** to a probe. 2. **Closed (2 marks):** The port is reachable but no service is listening. The target responds with an **RST (Reset)** packet. This indicates the host is online, but the specific port is unused. 3. **Filtered (2 marks):** A firewall or ACL is blocking probes to this port. The scanner does not receive any response, or receives an **ICMP Destination Unreachable**. The actual state cannot be determined. **(b) Angry IP Scanner Workflow (7 marks)** **Workflow steps (5 marks):** 1. Download and install Angry IP Scanner from `angryip.org` (or `advanced-ip-scanner.com`). 2. Launch the application. The GUI shows the main scanning window. 3. Enter the **IP range**, e.g., `192.168.1.1` to `192.168.1.254`. 4. Open **Tools → Preferences → Ports**. Choose a port range, e.g., `1-1024`. 5. In the **Fetchers** tab, enable *Ping, Hostname, MAC, NetBIOS*, and the specific port columns to be scanned. 6. Click the **Start** button. The tool performs multi-threaded ICMP pings, then port probes. 7. View the result table. Each row represents a host; columns show the port states and metadata. **Well-known port numbers (2 marks — 0.4 each):** - **HTTP** — **80** - **HTTPS** — **443** - **SSH** — **22** - **FTP** — **21** - **DNS** — **53** --- ### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Differentiate between Angry IP Scanner and Advanced IP Scanner. List any four features of each tool. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the role of port scanning in **network security auditing**. Describe how a system administrator would use these tools to harden a network. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Comparison: Angry IP Scanner vs Advanced IP Scanner (7 marks)** | Feature | Angry IP Scanner | Advanced IP Scanner | |---|---|---| | **License** | Open-source (**GPL v2**) | Proprietary freeware | | **Platforms** | Windows, Linux, macOS | Windows only | | **Engine** | Multi-threaded (very fast) | Single-threaded | | **Portability** | Portable (no install on Windows) | Requires installation | | **Remote Control** | Not available | Available via **Radmin** | | **Export Format** | CSV, TXT | CSV | | **Fingerprinting** | Basic (TTL, HTTP banner) | Basic (NetBIOS, services) | (4 marks for the table — 0.5 each for 8 unique points) **Additional features of Angry IP Scanner (1.5 marks — any 3):** - Java-based, cross-platform - Fetchers plugin architecture (HTTP, NetBIOS) - Command-line interface for scripting **Additional features of Advanced IP Scanner (1.5 marks — any 3):** - One-click remote desktop - Wake-on-LAN support - Auto-detection of network range from your current IP **(b) Role in Network Security Auditing (7 marks)** **Role (3 marks):** Port scanning helps auditors identify the **attack surface** of a network. By mapping all open ports and services, auditors can: - Detect **unauthorized services** (e.g., a rogue FTP server). - Identify **outdated software** vulnerable to exploits. - Verify **firewall rules** are working as intended. - Ensure **compliance** with security policies (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001). **How an admin hardens the network (4 marks):** 1. **Run regular scans** of the entire `/24` subnet. 2. **Close unnecessary ports** by stopping unused services (e.g., `systemctl stop telnet`). 3. **Patch and update** services found on open ports. 4. **Block ports at the firewall** if not in use. 5. **Configure IDS/IPS** to alert on scan attempts. 6. **Document and re-scan** after changes to verify hardening. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > - **Do NOT confuse** port scanning with **vulnerability scanning** (which includes actual exploit attempts). > - **Do NOT forget** to list the *three port states* (Open, Closed, Filtered) — this is a guaranteed 2-mark item in the marking scheme. > - **Do NOT claim** Angry IP Scanner performs *active OS fingerprinting* like Nmap. It only infers OS from **TTL values**. > - **Always mention** the legal angle: scanning networks you do **not own or have permission for** is illegal under the **IT Act, 2000 (India)** and the **Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (USA)**. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ **Port scanning** = probing ports to find services; states are **Open / Closed / Filtered**. - ✅ **Angry IP Scanner** = open-source, cross-platform, multi-threaded, GPL v2. - ✅ **Advanced IP Scanner** = Windows-only, freeware, has Radmin remote control. - ✅ Default technique used by both: **TCP Connect scan** (full 3-way handshake). - ✅ First step: **ICMP Echo Request (Ping)** to check host liveness. - ✅ Fallback ping: **TCP port 80** if ICMP is blocked. - ✅ Port range **0–65535**, well-known range **0–1023**. - ✅ Must-know ports: **21 (FTP), 22 (SSH), 23 (Telnet), 25 (SMTP), 53 (DNS), 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS), 3389 (RDP)**. - ✅ **OS detection via TTL:** Windows = 128, Linux = 64, Cisco = 255. - ✅ Formula to remember: $\text{Total Ports} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$. - ✅ Uses: reconnaissance, auditing, compliance, pentesting. - ✅ Lab procedure: Install → Set range → Pick ports → Enable fetchers → Start → Screenshot. - ✅ Output: tabular GUI with **IP, Ping, Hostname, Port state, MAC**. - ✅ Legal reminder: Always obtain **written authorization** before scanning. - ✅ Fetchers available: **Ping, Hostname, Port, HTTP banner, NetBIOS, MAC**. - ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme weightage: typically **Part A (3 marks) + Part B (7–14 marks)** in lab viva.

topics.title: Penetration testing and vulnerability scanning using Burp Suite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCL508 - NETWORK AND SYSTEM SECURITY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Penetration Testing (Pentesting)** is a structured, authorized, and systematic simulation of cyber-attacks against a computer system, network, or web application, performed to evaluate the security posture of the target by safely exploiting identified vulnerabilities. The goal is to identify weak points, determine the impact of a real-world breach, and provide actionable remediation guidance aligned with the **NIST SP 800-115** Technical Guide to Information Security Testing and the **OWASP Testing Framework v4.2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulnerability Scanning** is the automated, non-destructive process of probing a target system against a known database of Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (**CVE**), Common Weakness Enumeration (**CWE**), and the **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** catalogue, in order to enumerate, classify, and report security weaknesses without actively exploiting them. The standard scoring model employed is the **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS v3.1)**, which produces a base score in the range **0.0 – 10.0**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Burp Suite (PortSwigger Security)** is an integrated, GUI-based, intercepting **web application penetration testing platform** written in Java. It functions as a **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** HTTP/HTTPS proxy, allowing a security analyst to inspect, mutate, replay, fuzz, and bruteforce web traffic between a browser (client) and the target server, thereby exposing logic flaws, injection points, broken authentication, and session management weaknesses.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Inspector" Metaphor

Imagine your web application is a **newly constructed high-rise building**.

- **Vulnerability Scanning** is the **fire-safety and structural inspector** walking through the building with a clipboard and a checklist (the *CVE database*). He checks whether the doors are fire-rated, whether the smoke detectors are installed, and whether the emergency exits exist. He produces a report, but he does **not** try to break anything — he only *observes and lists* problems.
- **Penetration Testing** is the **ethical break-in specialist** hired by the building owner. He does not just read a checklist — he *actively tries* to pick the locks, jump the fences, bypass the elevators, and social-engineer the guard, to see **how far** an attacker can actually get. He also documents *how* he did it.
- **Burp Suite** is the **master toolkit** the ethical break-in specialist carries on his belt — containing lock-picks (Intruder), mirrors for peeking around corners (Proxy), recording devices (Repeater), and pattern-detection glasses (Sequencer).

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Evaluation** context, Experiment 2 expects the student to demonstrate the *operational workflow* of Burp Suite, perform at least one **passive scan** and one **active scan**, capture and modify at least one **HTTP request**, and document the **CVSS severity rating** of at least one detected vulnerability.

| CVE | MITRE Corporation | Unique identifier for a specific publicly known vulnerability (e.g., CVE-2021-44228) |

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Phase Penetration Testing Methodology (PTES / NIST SP 800-115)

The KTU 2024 syllabus maps the experiment to the following **structured kill-chain**:

1. **Phase 1 — Planning & Reconnaissance (Passive)**
   - Define scope, rules of engagement, authorization letter.
   - Gather OSINT (Open Source Intelligence): DNS records, subdomains, technology stack (e.g., Wappalyzer, `nmap`).
   - Identify entry points without touching the target actively.

2. **Phase 2 — Scanning & Enumeration (Active)**
   - Deploy Burp Suite's **Spider / Crawler** to map the application's URL tree.
   - Run **Passive Scanner** on intercepted traffic (zero impact, observes only).
   - Run **Active Scanner** to inject crafted payloads into parameters (SQLi, XSS probes).

3. **Phase 3 — Vulnerability Analysis & Exploitation**
   - Triage findings by **CVSS Base Score**.
   - Use **Repeater** to manually craft and replay modified HTTP requests.
   - Use **Intruder** to perform fuzzing, brute-force, and credential-stuffing attacks on input fields.
   - Confirm true positives, eliminate false positives.

4. **Phase 4 — Post-Exploitation & Reporting**
   - Document the *Impact* — data exfiltration, privilege escalation, lateral movement.
   - Map each finding to the **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** category.
   - Assign remediation priority based on the formula in § 2.3.

5. **Phase 5 — Remediation Verification (Re-Test)**
   - Re-run the active scan after developer patches.
   - Confirm the **CVSS score** drops to the acceptable threshold.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Vulnerability Scoring & Risk Quantification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 ESE viva voce and lab record writing. The KCVSS formula below is the **simplified base score** used at the lab level; the full CVSS v3.1 vector involves an Impact × Exploitability sub-formula published by FIRST.org.

$$
\text{CVSS Base} \;=\; \text{round}_{\text{up}}\bigl(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\; 10)\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{Risk Priority} \;=\; \text{Severity} \times \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Asset Value}
$$

$$
\text{Attack Surface Area} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl(V_{i} \times E_{i} \times C_{i}\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} T_{\text{detect},j}}{m}
$$

**Where:**
- $V_i$ = Vulnerability criticality of asset $i$ (CVSS / 10)
- $E_i$ = Exposure level of asset $i$ (0 = internal, 1 = public)
- $C_i$ = Sensitivity / criticality coefficient of asset $i$ (0.5 – 2.0)
- $T_{\text{detect},j}$ = detection latency (in hours) for incident $j$

> [!NOTE]
> The `$\vert$` symbol in any markdown table is intentionally replaced with `$\mid$` or `$\vert$` so as not to break the pipe-delimited table syntax. This is a **mandatory exam-handbook rule** for all KTU formula sheets.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the CVSS v3.1 Base Score (Full First.org Formula)

The full CVSS v3.1 base score is computed via a two-stage process: first the **Impact Sub-Score (ISS)**, then the **Impact**, then the **Exploitability**, then the Base.

**Step 1 — Impact Sub-Score (ISS)**

$$
\text{ISS} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \bigl[(1 - \text{Conf}) \times (1 - \text{Integ}) \times (1 - \text{Avail})\bigr]
$$

**Step 2 — Impact**

$$
\text{Impact} \;=\;
\begin{cases}
7.52 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.029) \;-\; 3.25 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if Scope Unchanged} \\
6.42 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.029) \;-\; 3.25 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if Scope Changed}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Exploitability**

$$
\text{Exploitability} \;=\; 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI}
$$

**Step 4 — Base Score**

$$
\text{Base} \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\text{round}_{\text{up}}\bigl(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\; 10)\bigr) & \text{if Impact} \le 0 \\
0 & \text{if Impact} = 0
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\text{round}_{\text{up}}$ means **round to one decimal place, rounding up** (e.g., 4.02 becomes 4.3, per CVSS v3.1 specification § 7.1). The metric weights $AV, AC, PR, UI$ are pulled from the CVSS vector string and are constants published by FIRST.org.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — CVSS Calculation for a Reflected XSS

Consider a **Reflected XSS** vulnerability in a search field of an e-commerce site.

**Given metrics:**
- Attack Vector (AV) = **Network** → weight = **0.85**
- Attack Complexity (AC) = **Low** → weight = **0.77**
- Privileges Required (PR) = **None** → weight = **0.85**
- User Interaction (UI) = **Required** → weight = **0.62**
- Confidentiality (Conf) = **Low (L)** → value = **0.22**
- Integrity (Integ) = **Low (L)** → value = **0.22**
- Availability (Avail) = **None (N)** → value = **0.0**
- Scope = **Unchanged**

**Step 1 — Compute ISS**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISS} &= 1 - \bigl[(1 - 0.22) \times (1 - 0.22) \times (1 - 0.0)\bigr] \\
&= 1 - \bigl[0.78 \times 0.78 \times 1.0\bigr] \\
&= 1 - 0.6084 \\
&= 0.3916
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Impact (Scope Unchanged branch)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 7.52 \times (0.3916 - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (0.3916 - 0.02)^{15} \\
&= 7.52 \times 0.3626 - 3.25 \times (0.3716)^{15} \\
&\approx 2.7267 - 3.25 \times 4.42 \times 10^{-7} \\
&\approx 2.7267 - 0.00000144 \\
&\approx 2.7267
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Exploitability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.62 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.62 \\
&\approx 2.2735
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Base Score**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base} &= \text{round}_{\text{up}}\bigl(\min(2.7267 + 2.2735,\; 10)\bigr) \\
&= \text{round}_{\text{up}}\bigl(\min(5.0002,\; 10)\bigr) \\
&= 5.1 \;\;\; \text{(Medium Severity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result **5.1** places this reflected XSS firmly in the **MEDIUM** CVSS band (4.0 – 6.9). This means the developer must patch it within the standard 30-day remediation window per most enterprise SLAs, but it is **not** a fire-fighting P0 critical ticket.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Burp Tool

| If your objective is… | Use this Burp Module | Because… |
|------------------------|----------------------|----------|
| Capture and modify a login request | **Proxy + Repeater** | Proxy captures; Repeater allows iterative modification |
| Find hidden endpoints on a website | **Spider / Crawler** | Recursively follows all `<a href>` and form actions |
| Brute-force 1000 passwords on one parameter | **Intruder** | Sniper / Battering-ram / Pitchfork / Cluster-bomb attack types |
| Verify CSRF token randomness | **Sequencer** | Performs FIPS statistical randomness test on captured tokens |
| Decode a suspicious JWT in a Cookie header | **Decoder** | Base64-URL decodes the header and payload segments |
| Compare two responses side-by-side | **Comparer** | Highlights byte-level / word-level differences |
| Confirm blind SQL injection via DNS lookup | **Collaborator** | Generates unique callback domain; checks for out-of-band hit |
| Test for IDOR in `/api/users/1234` | **Repeater + Intruder** | Manually increment `id` parameter; fuzz for valid ranges |

### 3.4 The Exhaustive Request-Response Flow Through Burp Proxy

A single HTTP request passes through **seven conceptual stages** when Burp Proxy is intercepting:

1. **Browser** generates `GET /search?q=phones HTTP/1.1` with full headers.
2. **OS TCP stack** wraps it in TCP+IP packets to destination port 443.
3. **Burp Proxy** (listening on `127.0.0.1:8080`) **intercepts** the request because the browser is proxy-configured to localhost.
4. **Burp UI** displays the request in the *Intercept* tab. The user can **Edit**, **Drop**, or **Forward**.
5. **Burp** opens a **new TCP connection** to the real target server (`testphp.vulnweb.com:80`).
6. **Target server** processes the (possibly modified) request and returns an HTTP response.
7. **Burp** intercepts the response, logs it in *HTTP History*, and (if *Intercept Response* is on) pauses for user inspection before delivery to the browser.

> [!NOTE]
> For HTTPS, Burp installs a **per-session CA certificate** (downloadable at `http://burp/cert`) into the browser's trust store. This is called **SSL Inspection / TLS Termination at Proxy** and is the technical reason HTTPS traffic can be inspected in cleartext inside Burp's UI.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question Bank]`

> Define the term **vulnerability scanning** as per NIST SP 800-115. Differentiate it from **penetration testing** in two clear points.

**Model Answer:**

> Vulnerability scanning is the *automated, non-destructive* identification of security weaknesses in a target system by comparing its configuration and version strings against a known database of CVEs, CWEs, and vendor advisories. The scan *stops at detection* and produces a ranked list of findings classified by CVSS v3.1 severity bands (None, Low, Medium, High, Critical).
>
> **Difference from Penetration Testing:**
>
> - **Depth:** Vulnerability scanning identifies *that* a weakness exists; penetration testing demonstrates *how* it can be exploited and what data/privileges are at stake.
> - **Technique:** Scanning uses pre-canned signature probes (e.g., Nessus, Burp Passive Scanner); pentesting uses creative, manual logic-chains (e.g., Burp Repeater + Intruder + chained XSS-to-CSRF).
> - **Output:** Scanning yields a *list*; pentesting yields a *story* (the attack narrative) plus business-impact analysis.
> - **Risk:** Scanning is safe to run in production; pentesting carries a controlled risk of service disruption and requires an explicit **Rules of Engagement** document.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]`

> List **any six** core modules of Burp Suite and state the operational purpose of each in one line.

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Proxy** — Intercepts and logs HTTP/HTTPS traffic between browser and target.
> 2. **Repeater** — Manually crafts, modifies, and replays individual HTTP requests.
> 3. **Intruder** — Performs automated fuzzing and brute-forcing of marked parameter positions.
> 4. **Scanner** — Runs passive observation plus active payload-based vulnerability detection.
> 5. **Sequencer** — Statistically analyses the randomness of session tokens and cookies.
> 6. **Decoder** — Encodes and decodes data formats such as Base64, URL, HTML, and Hex.
> 7. **Comparer** — Performs a byte-level or word-level diff between two pieces of data.
> 8. **Collaborator** — Detects out-of-band vulnerabilities such as blind SSRF and blind SQLi.
