**Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution:**

> Given vector: `AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:N`
>
> **Step 1 — Identify metric weights from FIRST.org table:**
> - AV:N → **0.85**, AC:L → **0.77**, PR:N → **0.85**, UI:R → **0.62**
> - C:H → **0.56**, I:H → **0.56**, A:N → **0.0**
> - Scope: **Changed** → S:C
>
> **Step 2 — Compute ISS**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISS} &= 1 - \bigl[(1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.0)\bigr] \\
&= 1 - \bigl[0.44 \times 0.44 \times 1.0\bigr] \\
&= 1 - 0.1936 \\
&= 0.8064
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3 — Compute Impact (Scope Changed branch)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 6.42 \times (0.8064 - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (0.8064 - 0.02)^{15} \\
&= 6.42 \times 0.7774 - 3.25 \times (0.7864)^{15} \\
&\approx 4.9909 - 3.25 \times 0.0313 \\
&\approx 4.9909 - 0.1017 \\
&\approx 4.8892
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 4 — Compute Exploitability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.62 \\
&\approx 2.2735
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 5 — Compute Base Score**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base} &= \text{round}_{\text{up}}\bigl(\min(4.8892 + 2.2735,\; 10)\bigr) \\
&= \text{round}_{\text{up}}(7.1627) \\
&= 7.2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Severity Band:** **HIGH (7.0 – 8.9)** — must be patched within the 14-day enterprise SLA window.
>
> **OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:** **A03:2021 — Injection**
>
> **Standard Remediation:**
> 1. **Output Encoding** — encode `<`, `>`, `"`, `'`, `&` in HTML context (use OWASP ESAPI or framework-native encoders).
> 2. **Context-Aware Sanitization** — use `DOMPurify` library for rich HTML content.
> 3. **Content Security Policy (CSP)** — deploy strict `script-src 'self'` and `default-src 'none'`.
> 4. **HTTPOnly + Secure** flags on session cookies to block `document.cookie` exfiltration.
> 5. **Input Validation** — allow-list only expected characters (e.g., regex `^[A-Za-z0-9 .,!?]{1,500}$`).

*(2 marks Phase table + 1 mark Step 1 metric extraction + 1.5 marks Step 2 ISS + 1.5 marks Step 3 Impact + 1 mark Step 4 Exploitability + 1 mark Step 5 Base + 1 mark OWASP mapping + 0.5 mark remediation identification = approx 7 marks)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Practical / Lab-Focused

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Retest]`
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the **operational architecture of Burp Suite** as a Man-in-the-Middle proxy. Explain how HTTPS interception is achieved despite the protocol being end-to-end encrypted.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** During a lab exercise on `http://testphp.vulnweb.com`, the Burp **Active Scanner** reports an **SQL Injection** in the `artist` parameter. Demonstrate, with a **step-by-step Burp Suite workflow**, how you would confirm this finding using **Repeater** and how you would **classify the finding** under the **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** with proper **CVSS v3.1 severity rating**.

##### MODEL ANSWER — Part B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution:**

> The Burp Suite Man-in-the-Middle architecture (refer to the mermaid diagram in Section 4.1) consists of:
>
> 1. **Browser** (e.g., Firefox with FoxyProxy) is configured with `HTTP Proxy = 127.0.0.1`, `Port = 8080`.
> 2. **Burp Proxy Listener** on `127.0.0.1:8080` accepts every outgoing request from the browser.
> 3. **Intercept Engine** pauses the request so the analyst can **Edit / Drop / Forward**.
> 4. **Burp opens a new TLS connection** to the real server on behalf of the browser.
> 5. The server's response is also captured and logged in **HTTP History** before delivery to the browser.
>
> **How HTTPS Interception Works (despite end-to-end encryption):**
>
> 1. Burp generates a **per-session RSA CA certificate** (downloadable from `http://burp/cert`).
> 2. The browser is configured to **trust this Burp CA** as a root certificate (typically in Firefox's *Certificate Manager → Authorities*).
> 3. When the browser sends a request to `https://target.com`, Burp **terminates the TLS connection** locally and presents its own dynamically-generated leaf certificate signed by the Burp CA — which the browser *trusts*.
> 4. Burp then opens a *separate, legitimate* TLS connection to the real `target.com`.
> 5. Inside Burp, the **plaintext HTTP** is visible to the analyst, and modifications can be injected before re-encryption to the real server.
>
> *(3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for MITM flow + 2 marks for HTTPS interception mechanism = 7 marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Configure Browser & Burp Proxy:** Set Firefox proxy to `127.0.0.1:8080`; install Burp CA in Firefox; ensure Burp **Intercept is ON**.
>
> **Step 2 — Capture the Request:** Browse to `http://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1`; Burp intercepts the GET request. The `artist=1` parameter is highlighted.
>
> **Step 3 — Send to Repeater:** Right-click the intercepted request → **"Send to Repeater"** → switch to *Repeater* tab.
>
> **Step 4 — Inject Test Payloads:** In Repeater, modify the `artist` parameter sequentially:
>
> - `artist=1'` → server returns **SQL error** (e.g., `You have an error in your SQL syntax`) → confirms String-based SQLi.
> - `artist=1' OR '1'='1` → returns **all rows** (true positive confirmation).
> - `artist=1' UNION SELECT 1,2,3-- -` → enumerate column count via union injection.
> - `artist=1' UNION SELECT user(),database(),version()-- -` → **data exfiltration** confirmed.
>
> **Step 5 — Classify the Finding:**
> - **OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:** **A03:2021 — Injection** (sub-type: SQL Injection)
> - **CWE Reference:** **CWE-89 — Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command**
> - **CVSS v3.1 Vector (Network, Low complexity, No priv, No UI, Unchanged scope, High Conf + High Integ + No Avail):**
>   `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N`
>
> **Step 6 — Severity Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISS} &= 1 - [(1-0.56)\times(1-0.56)\times(1-0)] = 0.8064 \\
\text{Impact (S:U)} &= 7.52 \times (0.8064-0.029) - 3.25\times(0.8064-0.02)^{15} \\
&\approx 5.8490 - 0.1017 = 5.7473 \\
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \approx 4.5675 \\
\text{Base} &= \text{round}_{\text{up}}\bigl(\min(5.7473 + 4.5675,\; 10)\bigr) = \text{round}_{\text{up}}(10) = 10.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** **CRITICAL (9.0 – 10.0)** — must be patched as a **P0 fire-fighting ticket within 24 hours**.
>
> **Standard Remediation:** Use **parameterized queries / prepared statements**, ORMs (Hibernate, SQLAlchemy), allow-list input validation, principle of least privilege on DB user, and deploy a **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** such as ModSecurity with OWASP CRS.

*(1 mark Step 1–3 setup + 2 marks Step 4 payload logic + 1 mark Step 5 OWASP/CWE mapping + 2 marks Step 6 CVSS calc + 1 mark remediation = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Active Scan with Exploit Run (–2 marks):** Active Scanner *probes* but does not exploit. Students who claim "Active Scanner performs the attack" lose marks. Only **Intruder** and **Collaborator** actually exploit.
> 2. **Forgetting the `round up` rule in CVSS (–1 mark):** CVSS v3.1 mandates `round up` to one decimal. Writing "4.02" instead of "4.3" is a marking penalty.
> 3. **Wrong Scope Branch (–2 marks):** Using the *Unchanged* impact formula when scope is *Changed* gives a wrong Base score. Always check the `S:C` vs `S:U` flag.
> 4. **Mixing up Proxy and Repeater (–1 mark):** Proxy *captures*; Repeater *replays & modifies*. They are NOT the same module.
> 5. **Wrong OWASP year (–1 mark):** Quoting OWASP Top 10 (2017) instead of (2021) costs a mark. KTU 2024 syllabus is aligned to **2021 edition**.
> 6. **Skipping Rules of Engagement:** In the lab record, students forget to mention the **authorization letter and scope** before running scans. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing this professional-ethics step.
> 7. **Confusing CWE with CVE (–1 mark):** CWE = *type* of weakness (e.g., CWE-89); CVE = *specific instance* (e.g., CVE-2024-12345). Both must be mentioned.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

**Core Concepts to Memorize:**

- **Pentest ≠ Vuln Scan.** Scan = *detect*; Pentest = *detect + exploit + impact-story*.
- **Burp default proxy:** `127.0.0.1:8080`; **Burp CA cert:** `http://burp/cert`.
- **CVSS v3.1 Range:** `0.0` (None) → `3.9` (Low) → `6.9` (Medium) → `8.9` (High) → `10.0` (Critical).
- **CVSS Impact Formula Branches:** `7.52 × (ISS − 0.029) − 3.25 × (ISS − 0.02)^{15}` for **Scope Unchanged**; `6.42 × (ISS − 0.029) − 3.25 × (ISS − 0.02)^{15}` for **Scope Changed**.
- **Exploitability formula:** `8.22 × AV × AC × PR × UI`.
- **Base score:** `round up of min(Impact + Exploitability, 10)`; equals `0.0` if `Impact ≤ 0`.
- **Round up rule:** `4.02 → 4.3`, `7.16 → 7.2`, `9.95 → 10.0`.

**OWASP Top 10 (2021) — Mnemonic "BACIV IVIL S":**

1. **B**roken Access Control (A01)
2. **A**02 — Cryptographic Failures
3. **C**ryptographic Failures — wait, redo: **A03 — Injection** (SQLi, XSS, Command Inj.)
4. **A**04 — Insecure Design
5. **A**05 — Security Misconfiguration
6. **A**06 — Vulnerable and Outdated Components
7. **A**07 — Identification and Authentication Failures
8. **A**08 — Software and Data Integrity Failures
9. **A**09 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
10. **A**10 — Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

**Burp Module Quick-Ref:**

- **Proxy** = Intercepts HTTP/S traffic
- **Repeater** = Manual request replayer & editor
- **Intruder** = Fuzzing / Brute-force engine
- **Sequencer** = Token randomness analyzer
- **Decoder** = Encoding/decoding (Base64, URL, Hex)
- **Comparer** = Diff two responses
- **Scanner** = Passive + Active vulnerability probes
- **Collaborator** = OOB callback server (blind SSRF/SQLi)
- **Target / Site Map** = URL tree
- **HTTP History** = Chronological log

**Lab Viva One-Liners to Practice:**

- "I configured FoxyProxy to direct traffic to Burp's listener at port 8080."
- "I installed Burp's CA certificate into Firefox's *Authorities* trust store to allow HTTPS interception."
- "I sent the captured request to Repeater and injected `1' OR '1'='1` to confirm SQL injection."
- "The CVSS Base Score of 9.8 places this in the **Critical** band, mapped to **A03:2021 Injection** under OWASP Top 10."
- "Remediation: use parameterized queries, allow-list input validation, and deploy ModSecurity WAF with OWASP Core Rule Set."

**Ethics & Compliance Reminder (Mandatory for Lab Record Preface):**

> All penetration testing was performed under a written **Authorization Letter** from the system owner, on a **legally sanctioned target** (e.g., `testphp.vulnweb.com`, DVWA, HackTheBox, or in-house lab VM), in accordance with the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (India) § 66** and the **CERT-In Directions of 2022**. Any unauthorized testing is a punishable offence.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module Experiment 2: Penetration Testing and Vulnerability Scanning using Burp Suite**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — Password Cracking using John the Ripper / Hydra

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Password Cracking — Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Password Cracking** is the cryptographic and computational process of recovering plaintext passwords from data that has been transmitted or stored in a hashed, encrypted, or obfuscated form. It is performed using a combination of heuristic, statistical, and brute-force algorithmic techniques to bypass or defeat authentication mechanisms.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, password cracking is studied as a **defensive-security laboratory exercise** — meaning students perform it in **isolated, legally sanctioned lab environments** to:
- Understand attacker methodologies.
- Audit the strength of password policies.
- Evaluate hash-storage practices of real systems.

### 1.2 John the Ripper (JtR) — Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **John the Ripper** is an open-source, command-line password security auditing and recovery tool. It was originally designed for **offline cracking of hashed passwords** extracted from `/etc/shadow`, SAM databases, ZIP archives, and cryptographic containers.

- Original Author: **Solar Designer (Openwall Project)** — first released in **1996**.
- Latest Stable Branch: **John the Ripper Jumbo (community-enhanced)**.
- Primary Function: **Offline hash recovery** (post-compromise scenario).
- Hash Support: Over **200+ hash formats** including MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512, LM, NTLM, bcrypt, and more.

### 1.3 Hydra — Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Hydra (THC-Hydra)** is a parallelized, network-login cracker developed by **The Hacker's Choice (THC)**. It performs **online password guessing attacks** against live network services.

- Primary Function: **Online authentication brute-forcing** over the network.
- Protocols Supported: SSH, FTP, HTTP-POST/GET, Telnet, SMB, RDP, MySQL, PostgreSQL, VNC, LDAP, and **50+ other services**.
- Key Property: **Highly parallelized** — supports multiple concurrent connections.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a giant keyring with millions of keys.** You have a locked safe but don't know which key fits. Instead of trying them one by one, you hand the keyring to a robot that tests each key in milliseconds.

- **John the Ripper** is like taking the **inside mechanism of the safe apart** (extracting the hash), and then having the robot try all the keys on a **workbench in your garage** — fast, private, no one watching.
- **Hydra** is like trying the keys **directly on the safe's keypad**, one after another, while a guard (the server) watches. If you try too fast, the guard locks you out.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in Password Cracking
- **Hash Rate (H/s)**: Hashes computed per second.
- **Charset Size (C)**: Number of possible characters in the pool.
- **Keyspace (K)**: Total combinations = $C^{L}$, where $L$ is the password length.
- **Cracking Time (T)**: Time to exhaust the search space.
- **Password Entropy (E)**: Bits of unpredictability.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — How Password Cracking Works

The general pipeline for any password cracking attack consists of **four logical stages**:

- **Stage 1 — Target Acquisition**
  - Offline mode (JtR): Dump `/etc/shadow`, SAM, or extract hashes from a stolen database file.
  - Online mode (Hydra): Identify the live service endpoint (host + port + protocol).

- **Stage 2 — Wordlist / Candidate Generation**
  - Dictionary attack: Try words from a curated list (e.g., `rockyou.txt`).
  - Brute-force attack: Generate every possible character combination.
  - Hybrid attack: Apply rule-based mutations (e.g., `password` → `P@ssw0rd!2024`).
  - Mask attack: Try candidates matching a known pattern (e.g., `?u?l?l?l?d?d?s`).

- **Stage 3 — Hashing & Comparison**
  - Compute hash($candidate$) using the same algorithm as the target.
  - Compare with the target hash byte-by-byte.
  - If a match is found → **plaintext is recovered**.

- **Stage 4 — Reporting & Logging**
  - Output the cracked password and its associated username.
  - Tools such as JtR write results into `~/.john/john.pot`.

### 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How'

| Aspect | Why It Matters | How It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Hashing** | Makes stored passwords unreadable to humans. | One-way function: $H = \text{hash}(P)$. Infeasible to invert. |
| **Salting** | Defeats precomputed rainbow tables. | Random string appended before hashing: $H = \text{hash}(P + S)$. |
| **Key Stretching** | Slows down attackers. | Iterated hashing (e.g., bcrypt, PBKDF2). |
| **Rate Limiting** | Stops online brute force. | Hydra must respect `wait` and `tasks` parameters. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cracking Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these formulas. They appear frequently in KTU 2024 ESE Module questions.

$$
E = L \cdot \log_{2}(C)
$$

$$
K = C^{L}
$$

$$
T = \frac{K}{R} \quad \text{(seconds)}
$$

$$
T_{\text{real}} = \frac{K \cdot C_{h}}{R}
$$

$$
P_{\text{crack}}(t) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{K}\right)^{R \cdot t}
$$

### 2.4 Explanation of Variables (No Pipes Used)

- $E$ = Password entropy in **bits**.
- $L$ = Password length in **characters**.
- $C$ = Size of character pool (e.g., 26 for lowercase letters, 95 for full ASCII printable set).
- $K$ = Total keyspace = number of possible passwords.
- $R$ = Cracking rate in **candidates per second**.
- $C_{h}$ = Cost of computing **one** hash (in seconds).
- $t$ = Elapsed attack time (in seconds).
- $T_{\text{real}}$ = Realistic time including per-hash computation cost.
- $P_{\text{crack}}(t)$ = Probability of successful crack within time $t$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility
- **Enterprise Security Audits**: Penetration testers verify weak corporate passwords.
- **Digital Forensics**: Investigators recover suspect passwords from seized drives.
- **Red Team Operations**: Simulate adversary behavior to test blue-team detection.
- **Compliance Validation**: PCI-DSS, ISO 27001, and NIST SP 800-63B mandate periodic password-strength testing.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Password Entropy

We start from the **information-theoretic definition** of entropy for a uniformly random character from a pool of size $C$:

$$
H_{\text{per-char}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{1}{C} \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{1}{C}\right)
$$

Since each character is equally likely with probability $\frac{1}{C}$, this simplifies as:

$$
H_{\text{per-char}} = -C \cdot \frac{1}{C} \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{1}{C}\right)
$$

The factor $C \cdot \frac{1}{C}$ equals **1**, leaving:

$$
H_{\text{per-char}} = \log_{2}(C)
$$

For a password of length $L$ where each character is **independent and uniformly random**, total entropy is the sum:

$$
E = L \cdot \log_{2}(C)
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each character contributes $\log_{2}(C)$ bits of information. Multiplying by $L$ characters yields the total entropy in bits.

### 3.2 Derivation of Keyspace Size

The total number of unique passwords of length $L$ over an alphabet of size $C$ is a combinatorial product:

$$
K = \underbrace{C \cdot C \cdot C \cdots C}_{L \text{ times}} = C^{L}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Position 1 has $C$ choices, position 2 has $C$ choices, …, position $L$ has $C$ choices. By the multiplication principle, the total is $C^{L}$.

### 3.3 Derivation of Brute-Force Cracking Time

If the attacker can test $R$ candidates per second, the **average** time to crack is half the keyspace:

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{K}{2 \cdot R}
$$

The **worst-case** time is the full keyspace:

$$
T_{\text{worst}} = \frac{K}{R}
$$

Accounting for per-hash cost $C_{h}$:

$$
T_{\text{real}} = \frac{K \cdot C_{h}}{R}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Total work (keyspace) divided by throughput (rate) gives time. Including $C_{h}$ models real CPU/GPU hashing latency.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** An 8-character password is drawn uniformly from the 95 printable ASCII characters. The attacker hashes at a rate of $R = 10^{10}$ hashes/sec (modern GPU). Compute entropy, keyspace, and worst-case cracking time.

**Step 1 — Entropy**

$$
E = L \cdot \log_{2}(C) = 8 \cdot \log_{2}(95)
$$

Computing $\log_{2}(95)$:

$$
\log_{2}(95) = \frac{\log_{10}(95)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.9777}{0.3010} \approx 6.5699
$$

Therefore:

$$
E \approx 8 \cdot 6.5699 \approx 52.56 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Keyspace**

$$
K = C^{L} = 95^{8}
$$

Computing $\log_{10}(K)$:

$$
\log_{10}(K) = 8 \cdot \log_{10}(95) = 8 \cdot 1.9777 \approx 15.8216
$$

Therefore:

$$
K \approx 10^{15.8216} \approx 6.63 \times 10^{15}
$$

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Cracking Time**

$$
T_{\text{worst}} = \frac{K}{R} = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{15}}{10^{10}} = 6.63 \times 10^{5} \text{ seconds}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
T_{\text{worst}} \approx \frac{6.63 \times 10^{5}}{3600} \approx 184.2 \text{ hours} \approx 7.67 \text{ days}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Standard SI conversions applied: $1 \text{ hr} = 3600 \text{ s}$, $1 \text{ day} = 24 \text{ hr}$.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Tool Comparison

| Feature | John the Ripper | Hydra |
|---|---|---|
| **Attack Mode** | Offline hash cracking | Online authentication brute-force |
| **Input Required** | Hash dump file | Live network service URL |
| **Speed** | Very high (GPU-accelerated via `john --format=raw-md5-opencl`) | Limited by server response and rate-limiting |
| **Stealth** | No network footprint | Detectable via logs and IDS |
| **Parallelism** | Multi-core CPU + OpenCL/CUDA | Multi-threaded concurrent connections |
| **Default Wordlist** | `/usr/share/john/password.lst` | User-supplied (often `rockyou.txt`) |
| **Detection Risk** | **Low** (local processing) | **High** (visible on the wire) |
| **Best Use Case** | Recovering hashes from stolen DB | Testing weak remote login credentials |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 John the Ripper — Operational Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Lab Administrator] --> B0[Create User Accounts]
    B0 --> C0[Generate Hash Dump from shadow file]
    C0 --> D0[Store hashes.txt in lab directory]
    D0 --> E0[Student Loads wordlist rockyou.txt]
    E0 --> F0[Execute john hashes.txt command]
    F0 --> G0{JtR Core Engine}
    G0 --> H0[Dictionary Mode]
    G0 --> I0[Rule-Based Mutations]
    G0 --> J0[Incremental Brute Force]
    G0 --> K0[Mask Attack]
    H0 --> L0[Hash Comparison Loop]
    I0 --> L0
    J0 --> L0
    K0 --> L0
    L0 --> M0{Match Found}
    M0 -- Yes --> N0[Write to john.pot file]
    M0 -- No --> O0[Try Next Candidate]
    O0 --> L0
    N0 --> P0[Display cracked credentials]
    P0 --> Q0[Student Submits Lab Record]
```

### 4.2 Hydra — Network Attack Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Student in Lab Environment] --> B1[Identify Target Service IP]
    B1 --> C1[Detect Protocol SSH FTP HTTP]
    C1 --> D1[Prepare username list users.txt]
    D1 --> E1[Prepare password wordlist]
    E1 --> F1[Execute hydra command with options]
    F1 --> G1[Establish Parallel TCP Connections]
    G1 --> H1[Send Login Request Attempt 1]
    H1 --> I1{Server Response}
    I1 -- HTTP 200 or Success --> J1[Valid Credential Found]
    I1 -- HTTP 401 or Fail --> K1[Try Next Password]
    K1 --> L1[Rotate Username or Advance Password]
    L1 --> H1
    J1 --> M1[Log cracked credentials]
    M1 --> N1[Generate Audit Report]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Password Attack Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X0[Available Password Hash] --> X1{Has Network Access?}
    X1 -- No Offline --> X2[Use John the Ripper]
    X1 -- Yes Online --> X3[Use Hydra]
    X2 --> X4{Hash Type Known?}
    X4 -- Yes MD5 SHA256 --> X5[Specify format flag]
    X4 -- No Unknown --> X6[Auto detect with john show]
    X5 --> X7[Choose Attack Mode]
    X6 --> X7
    X7 --> X7A[Wordlist mode]
    X7 --> X7B[Incremental mode]
    X7 --> X7C[Single crack mode]
    X7 --> X7D[Mask mode]
    X3 --> X8{Protocol Known?}
    X8 -- SSH --> X9A[hydra ssh module]
    X8 -- FTP --> X9B[hydra ftp module]
    X8 -- HTTP POST --> X9C[hydra http-post-form module]
```

### 4.4 Wordlist Generation & Hash Verification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Seed Wordlist password lst] --> S1[Apply Mangling Rules]
    S1 --> S2[Rule 1 Lowercase to Uppercase]
    S1 --> S3[Rule 2 Append digits 0 to 9]
    S1 --> S4[Rule 3 Prepend special chars]
    S1 --> S5[Rule 4 Combine words]
    S2 --> T0[Candidate Password Pool]
    S3 --> T0
    S4 --> T0
    S5 --> T0
    T0 --> U0[Hash Function MD5 SHA256]
    U0 --> V0[Compare Hash against Target]
    V0 --> W0{Match?}
    W0 -- Yes --> X0[Plaintext Recovered]
    W0 -- No --> T0
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between offline and online password cracking. Give one example tool for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Offline cracking** operates on extracted hash files without contacting the target system; it is faster and stealthier. Example: **John the Ripper**.
- **Online cracking** submits credential guesses directly to a live service; it is slower and visible to intrusion detection systems. Example: **Hydra (THC-Hydra)**.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a rainbow table and why does salting defeat it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **rainbow table** is a precomputed lookup dictionary mapping hashes to plaintexts.
- **Salting** appends a unique random string to each password before hashing, so identical passwords produce different hashes — making precomputed tables useless.

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of **John the Ripper**. List **four attack modes** supported by it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Working of John the Ripper:**
  1. Hashes are extracted from `/etc/shadow` or another source using `unshadow` or directly copied.
  2. The user invokes `john hashes.txt` in the terminal.
  3. JtR identifies the hash format automatically (or via `--format=`).
  4. Candidates are generated from the wordlist using **mangling rules**.
  5. Each candidate is hashed and compared with target hashes.
  6. On a match, the result is stored in `~/.john/john.pot`.
  7. Pressing any key shows the current cracking status.

- **Four Attack Modes (1.75 marks each):**
  1. **Single Crack Mode** — uses login names, GECOS fields, and usernames as candidate passwords.
  2. **Wordlist Mode** — iterates through a dictionary file applying mangling rules.
  3. **Incremental Mode** — exhaustive brute-force over a defined character class.
  4. **External / Mask Mode** — candidate generation following a user-defined pattern.

**(b)** A 6-character password is built from 26 lowercase letters. Compute its **entropy**, **keyspace**, and **average cracking time** assuming an attacker tests $10^{8}$ passwords per second. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$
E = L \cdot \log_{2}(C) = 6 \cdot \log_{2}(26)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(26) = \frac{\log_{10}(26)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.4150}{0.3010} \approx 4.7004
$$

$$
E \approx 6 \cdot 4.7004 \approx 28.20 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
K = C^{L} = 26^{6}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(K) = 6 \cdot 1.4150 = 8.490
$$

$$
K \approx 10^{8.49} \approx 3.09 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{K}{2R} = \frac{3.09 \times 10^{8}}{2 \cdot 10^{8}} \approx 1.545 \text{ seconds}
$$

> **Answer:** Entropy $\approx 28.20$ bits, Keyspace $\approx 3.09 \times 10^{8}$, Average cracking time $\approx 1.55$ seconds.

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working of **THC-Hydra** with a suitable block diagram. Mention **any three supported protocols**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Working of Hydra:**
  1. Identify the target IP and the running service (e.g., SSH on port 22).
  2. Provide a username list and a password wordlist.
  3. Hydra spawns multiple parallel threads, each attempting authentication.
  4. For each pair $(u, p)$, Hydra sends a login request and analyzes the response.
  5. Success indicators (HTTP 200, SSH banner matching, FTP 230) are flagged as a valid credential.
  6. Failed attempts are logged silently and the next password is attempted.
  7. Use the `-f` flag to stop on the first valid credential found.

- **Three Supported Protocols (1 mark each + 4 marks explanation = 7 total):**
  1. **SSH** — `hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt ssh://target`
  2. **FTP** — `hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt ftp://target`
  3. **HTTP-POST-FORM** — `hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt target http-post-form "/login:user=^USER^&pass=^PASS^:Invalid"`

**(b)** A password has length 10 drawn from a pool of 36 alphanumeric characters. Compute its **entropy**, **keyspace**, and **worst-case cracking time** if the cracking rate is $5 \times 10^{9}$ hashes/second. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$
E = L \cdot \log_{2}(C) = 10 \cdot \log_{2}(36)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(36) = \frac{\log_{10}(36)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.5563}{0.3010} \approx 5.1699
$$

$$
E \approx 10 \cdot 5.1699 \approx 51.70 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
K = C^{L} = 36^{10}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(K) = 10 \cdot 1.5563 = 15.563
$$

$$
K \approx 10^{15.563} \approx 3.65 \times 10^{15}
$$

$$
T_{\text{worst}} = \frac{K}{R} = \frac{3.65 \times 10^{15}}{5 \times 10^{9}} = 7.3 \times 10^{5} \text{ seconds}
$$

Converting to days:

$$
T_{\text{worst}} = \frac{7.3 \times 10^{5}}{86400} \approx 8.45 \text{ days}
$$

> **Answer:** Entropy $\approx 51.70$ bits, Keyspace $\approx 3.65 \times 10^{15}$, Worst-case time $\approx 8.45$ days.

> 1. **Forgetting to specify the hash format** in `john` loses 2 marks — always use `--format=`.
> 2. **Mixing up average vs worst-case time** in entropy/keyspace problems loses 2 marks — clearly write "average = K/2R" or "worst-case = K/R".
> 3. **Wrong unit conversions** (seconds vs hours vs days) — explicitly show the conversion step.
> 4. **Not citing the attack mode name** in JtR/Hydra questions loses 1 mark — name: Single Crack, Wordlist, Incremental, External.
> 5. **Confusing offline vs online context** — JtR is offline, Hydra is online. Mixing them costs 2 marks.

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **John the Ripper** = **offline**, hash-based, very fast, supports **200+ formats**.
- **Hydra** = **online**, network-based, parallelized, supports **50+ protocols**.
- **Dictionary attack** uses a curated wordlist (e.g., `rockyou.txt`).
- **Brute-force attack** exhausts the entire keyspace.
- **Hybrid attack** combines wordlists with mangling rules.
- **Mask attack** uses positional placeholders like `?u?l?l?d?d`.
- **Entropy formula:** $E = L \cdot \log_{2}(C)$ — memorise this line.
- **Keyspace formula:** $K = C^{L}$ — apply logarithm base 10 for huge numbers.
- **Time formulas:** $T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{K}{2R}$, $T_{\text{worst}} = \frac{K}{R}$.
- **Salting** defeats rainbow tables; **key stretching** (bcrypt) slows brute force.
- Always use **isolated lab VMs** — never run cracking tools against unauthorized systems.
- Default JtR wordlist path: `/usr/share/john/password.lst`.
- Default output file: `~/.john/john.pot`.
- Hydra `-f` flag = stop on first success.
- Hydra `-t N` flag = N parallel tasks (default 16).
